---
title: "Condition Number and Numerical Stability"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Ellipse

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # data / inputs / true answers
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # perturbed answers / the amplified error
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # highlights, exact references
GOLD   = "#d4a017"   # secondary curves
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def cond2(A):
    """2-norm condition number via the singular values, the definition this
    article derives — used here only to draw and label the pictures."""
    s = np.linalg.svd(A, compute_uv=False)
    return s.max() / s.min()
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the **numerical-reality** article that closes
> the conceptual build of the Geometry-of-Data track, just before the bridge
> [GEOM-27]. Everything in it is a *consequence* of machinery already on the table. It
> assumes you can read **eigenvalues and eigenvectors** as the directions a map only
> stretches, $Av = \lambda v$ ([GEOM-14]); that you know the **singular value
> decomposition** $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ — every matrix is *rotate, scale along orthogonal
> axes, rotate* — and that $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_{\max}$ ([GEOM-20]); and that you recognize a
> **positive-definite** matrix as one with all-positive eigenvalues whose quadratic form is
> a bowl ([GEOM-18]). The one question here: *why do some perfectly invertible problems give
> garbage answers on a computer, and what single number predicts it?* The answer — the
> **condition number** $\kappa(A) = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ — discharges
> **[REGR-07]**'s eigenvalue/condition-number prerequisite, explains why ridge regression
> and QR/SVD solves exist, and hands Optimization the quantity that governs how slowly
> gradient descent crawls across an elongated bowl.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A linear system $Ax = b$ can be perfectly solvable in exact arithmetic — $A$ invertible,
one clean answer — and still return nonsense on a real computer, because the data $b$ and
every intermediate number carry tiny errors, and some matrices *amplify* those errors
ferociously. This article derives the number that measures the amplification: the
**condition number**

$$
\kappa(A) = \|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\| = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\min}},
$$

the ratio of the largest to the smallest singular value. We prove it is the worst-case
factor by which a *relative* error in $b$ can be magnified in the solution $x$, read it
geometrically as the elongation of the ellipse $A$ makes from a sphere, and tie it
directly to **multicollinearity** in regression. The single claim defended throughout:
$\kappa$ is a property of the *problem*, not the algorithm — no clever code rescues an
ill-conditioned system, but a good algorithm refuses to make a well-conditioned one
worse, which is exactly why we **solve rather than invert**, prefer **QR/SVD** to the
normal equations, and never test singularity with the determinant.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The **condition number** $\kappa(A) = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ is the worst-case
factor by which solving $Ax = b$ can blow up a *relative* error. If your data carry a
relative error of $\varepsilon$ (and on a computer they always carry at least the
machine-precision floor $\varepsilon \approx 10^{-16}$), the answer can be wrong by a
relative amount up to $\kappa(A)\,\varepsilon$. A matrix with $\kappa = 10^{8}$ shreds
about half your significant digits *before any algorithm runs*. Geometrically, $\kappa$ is
how stretched-out an ellipse $A$ turns the unit sphere into — and a near-flat ellipse is
exactly what near-collinear columns (multicollinearity) look like.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The headline equation $\kappa = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$
will look like it falls out of a norm inequality, but by the end you should see it as
inevitable: $A$ stretches the most-amplified input direction by $\sigma_{\max}$ and the
least by $\sigma_{\min}$, so when we *invert* the map the roles swap, and the gap between
those two extremes is the whole story. Conditioning is geometry — the geometry of an
ellipse — wearing a number.

# Why we are here: a solvable problem that lies {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **why does invertibility — the textbook
guarantee of a unique answer — fail to guarantee a *usable* answer on a computer?** Every
step below answers some piece of that.

## The original problem

The story begins with the gap between *mathematics* and *arithmetic on a machine*. In the
1940s the first electronic computers were put to work solving large linear systems — for
ballistics, for structural engineering, for the emerging field of numerical weather
prediction. John von Neumann and Herman Goldstine, analyzing Gaussian elimination on these
machines in 1947, ran into a disquieting fact: a system could be perfectly nonsingular and
yet the computed solution could be wildly wrong, with the error growing with the *size* and
the *shape* of the matrix in a way that pure linear algebra had never needed to name.

The trouble is that a computer does not store real numbers. It stores **floating-point**
approximations — a fixed budget of significant digits (about 16 decimal digits in the
standard `double` format) — so almost every number, including the entries of $b$ and every
sum and product formed along the way, is rounded. The question von Neumann forced into the
open is: *given that the inputs are slightly wrong, how wrong can the output be?* For some
matrices the answer is "about as wrong as the inputs"; for others it is "catastrophically
more wrong." Alan Turing, in 1948, named the multiplier — he introduced **condition
numbers** for matrices and showed they govern exactly this amplification. The number we
derive below is his.

This is the *why* we hold onto: a problem can be theoretically solvable and numerically
hopeless, and the two facts live in different worlds. Invertibility is a yes/no question
about exact arithmetic. Conditioning is a *how-much* question about the inevitable errors
of finite-precision arithmetic, and it is the one that decides whether you can trust the
answer your computer printed.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Isn't 'invertible' enough? If $A^{-1}$ exists, can't I always recover $x$?"* In exact
arithmetic, yes — $x = A^{-1}b$ is the unique answer. But you never have exact arithmetic,
and you rarely have an exact $b$. The right mental model is that you are handed not $b$ but
$b + \delta b$ for some small unknown perturbation $\delta b$, and you compute not with
$A^{-1}$ but with a rounded version of it. Invertibility says the *map* $b \mapsto x$ is
well-defined; conditioning says how violently that map magnifies the uncertainty you could
not avoid. A matrix can be invertible and still have $\kappa(A) = 10^{15}$ — invertible in
name, useless in practice.
:::

# Intuition before symbols: the ellipse {#sec-intuition}

Before any norms, picture what a matrix *does* to error. From [GEOM-20] we know the single
cleanest fact about any matrix: it sends the **unit sphere to an ellipse** (an ellipsoid in
higher dimensions). The SVD spells out how — $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ takes a unit input,
rotates it ($V^\top$), stretches it along orthogonal axes by the singular values
$\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \dots > 0$ ($\Sigma$), and rotates again ($U$). The longest
semi-axis of the resulting ellipse has length $\sigma_{\max}$; the shortest has length
$\sigma_{\min}$.

Now overlay the two questions conditioning cares about.

- **Forward:** if I wiggle the input $x$ by a tiny vector, how much can $Ax$ wiggle? At
  most by $\sigma_{\max}$ times the wiggle — the most $A$ can stretch any direction.
- **Backward (the one we want):** I am solving $Ax = b$, so I am applying $A^{-1}$ to $b$.
  And $A^{-1}$ sends the ellipse *back* to the sphere — it stretches the flat direction of
  the ellipse (the $\sigma_{\min}$ axis) by a factor of $1/\sigma_{\min}$, which is **huge**
  when $\sigma_{\min}$ is tiny.

So a wiggle in $b$ that happens to land along the *flat* direction of $A$'s ellipse gets
blown up by $1/\sigma_{\min}$ when we solve. A wiggle along the *long* direction barely
moves. The ratio between the worst case and the best case — between the most stretched and
the least stretched — is $\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min} = \kappa(A)$. A round ellipse
($\kappa \approx 1$) treats every direction fairly: error in, comparable error out. A
cigar-thin ellipse (huge $\kappa$) has a direction along which solving is a precision
catastrophe. @fig-ellipse draws both.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ellipse
#| fig-cap: 'Conditioning is the shape of the ellipse. Each panel shows the unit circle (grey) and its image $A(\text{circle})$ (an ellipse). **Left:** a well-conditioned matrix ($\kappa \approx 1.5$) — a nearly round ellipse; the longest and shortest semi-axes (red and green, the singular directions scaled by $\sigma_{\max},\sigma_{\min}$) are comparable, so $A$ stretches every direction by roughly the same amount. **Right:** an ill-conditioned matrix ($\kappa \approx 40$) — a thin cigar; the flat direction is squashed by $\sigma_{\min}$, so *inverting* (solving $Ax=b$) must blow that direction back up by $1/\sigma_{\min}$, magnifying any error that points along it.'
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 240)
circle = np.vstack([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])

# Build two matrices with prescribed singular values via U Sigma V^T.
def make(s1, s2, ang_v=25, ang_u=15):
    cv, sv = np.cos(np.deg2rad(ang_v)), np.sin(np.deg2rad(ang_v))
    cu, su = np.cos(np.deg2rad(ang_u)), np.sin(np.deg2rad(ang_u))
    V = np.array([[cv, -sv], [sv, cv]])
    U = np.array([[cu, -su], [su, cu]])
    return U @ np.diag([s1, s2]) @ V.T

A_good = make(1.5, 1.0)
A_bad  = make(4.0, 0.1)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))
for ax, A, ttl in [(axes[0], A_good, "well-conditioned"),
                   (axes[1], A_bad,  "ill-conditioned")]:
    img = A @ circle
    ax.plot(circle[0], circle[1], color=GREY, lw=1.4, ls="--", label="unit circle")
    ax.plot(img[0], img[1], color=VEC, lw=2.4, label="image ellipse $A\\cdot$circle")
    U, s, Vt = np.linalg.svd(A)
    # long and short semi-axes of the ellipse: columns of U scaled by sigma
    long_ax = U[:, 0] * s[0]
    short_ax = U[:, 1] * s[1]
    ax.annotate("", xy=long_ax, xytext=(0, 0),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=HILITE, lw=2.6))
    ax.annotate("", xy=short_ax, xytext=(0, 0),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.6))
    ax.text(*(long_ax*1.08), r"$\sigma_{\max}$", color=HILITE, fontsize=12)
    ax.text(*(short_ax*1.25 + np.array([0.05, 0.05])), r"$\sigma_{\min}$",
            color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_title(f"{ttl}:  $\\kappa = {cond2(A):.1f}$")
    ax.set_xlim(-4.6, 4.6); ax.set_ylim(-2.6, 2.6)
axes[0].legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the condition number a property of the matrix or of the algorithm?"* Of the **matrix
— the problem itself.** It measures how sensitive the *true* answer is to perturbations in
the *true* data, with no computer in sight. This is the single most important conceptual
point in the article: $\kappa$ is the **conditioning** of the problem; how much *extra*
error a particular algorithm piles on top is the separate notion of **stability** (which we
get to in @sec-stability). No algorithm, however clever, can make an ill-conditioned
problem well-conditioned — the sensitivity is baked into $A$. The most a good algorithm can
promise is to not make things *worse* than $\kappa$ already forces.
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

To turn the ellipse picture into a theorem we need one tool — a way to measure the "size" of
a vector and the "size" of a matrix — and we build it from the floor.

## A two-minute refresher on norms {#sec-norms}

The length of a vector $x = (x_1,\dots,x_n)$ is the **Euclidean norm**, the
$n$-dimensional Pythagoras built in [GEOM-04]:

$$
\|x\| = \sqrt{x_1^2 + \dots + x_n^2} = \sqrt{x^\top x}.
$$ {#eq-vecnorm}

We will measure errors *relative*, as $\|\delta x\| / \|x\|$ — "what fraction of the
answer's length is error" — because that is the dimensionless quantity that compares fairly
across problems of different scales (an absolute error of $0.01$ is tiny if $\|x\| = 10^6$
and catastrophic if $\|x\| = 10^{-6}$).

To say how much a matrix can stretch a vector we need a matrix size, and the right one for
this job is the **(induced) 2-norm** — the largest factor by which $A$ can lengthen any
nonzero vector:

::: {#def-matnorm}
**Definition (matrix 2-norm).** For a matrix $A$,

$$
\|A\| = \max_{x \neq 0} \frac{\|Ax\|}{\|x\|}.
$$ {#eq-matnorm}
:::

Read @eq-matnorm literally: over every input direction, find the one $A$ stretches most,
and report that stretch factor. Two consequences fall out immediately, and we use both
constantly.

First, **the defining inequality**. Since $\|A\|$ is the *maximum* of $\|Ax\|/\|x\|$ over
all $x$, every individual ratio is at most $\|A\|$, which rearranges to

$$
\|Ax\| \le \|A\|\,\|x\| \qquad \text{for all } x.
$$ {#eq-submult}

This is just "no direction is stretched by more than the maximum stretch" — true by the
definition of maximum, nothing deeper.

Second, **the 2-norm is the largest singular value**. We imported this from [GEOM-20], but
here is the complete chain so nothing is assumed. Write the SVD $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ with
$U, V$ orthonormal and $\Sigma = \operatorname{diag}(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_n)$,
$\sigma_1 \ge \dots \ge \sigma_n \ge 0$. Orthonormal matrices preserve length — for any
$z$, $\|Uz\|^2 = (Uz)^\top(Uz) = z^\top U^\top U z = z^\top z = \|z\|^2$, since
$U^\top U = I$. So lengthening happens *only* in the $\Sigma$ step:

$$
\|Ax\| = \|U\Sigma V^\top x\| = \|\Sigma V^\top x\|.
$$

Substitute $z = V^\top x$; because $V^\top$ is orthonormal, $\|z\| = \|x\|$, and as $x$
ranges over all nonzero vectors so does $z$. Then

$$
\frac{\|Ax\|}{\|x\|} = \frac{\|\Sigma z\|}{\|z\|}
= \frac{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 z_1^2 + \dots + \sigma_n^2 z_n^2}}{\sqrt{z_1^2 + \dots + z_n^2}}.
$$

The numerator is a weighted length with weights $\sigma_i^2$. It is largest, relative to
the denominator, when all the "weight" of $z$ sits on the largest $\sigma_i$ — i.e. when
$z$ points along the first coordinate, $z = (1,0,\dots,0)$, giving the ratio $\sigma_1$. No
$z$ can beat it because every $\sigma_i^2 \le \sigma_1^2$. Therefore

$$
\|A\| = \sigma_{\max}.
$$ {#eq-norm-is-sigmax}

The same machinery gives $\|A^{-1}\|$, but first we need the SVD of $A^{-1}$ itself — and it
falls right out of the SVD of $A$. Inverting $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ reverses the three factors
and inverts each (using $(BC)^{-1} = C^{-1}B^{-1}$, and that for orthonormal $U$,
$U^{-1} = U^\top$):

$$
A^{-1} = (U\Sigma V^\top)^{-1} = (V^\top)^{-1}\Sigma^{-1}U^{-1} = V\,\Sigma^{-1}U^\top,
$$

where $\Sigma^{-1} = \operatorname{diag}(1/\sigma_1,\dots,1/\sigma_n)$ because inverting a
diagonal matrix just inverts each diagonal entry. Now $V\Sigma^{-1}U^\top$ is *almost* in SVD
form — $V$ and $U^\top$ are orthonormal — except the diagonal entries $1/\sigma_1 \le \dots
\le 1/\sigma_n$ are in *increasing* order, whereas singular values must be listed largest
first. So the singular values of $A^{-1}$ are exactly the reciprocals $1/\sigma_i$ reordered
largest-first, i.e. $1/\sigma_n \ge \dots \ge 1/\sigma_1$, and the largest of them is the
reciprocal of the *smallest* $\sigma_i$. Applying @eq-norm-is-sigmax to $A^{-1}$ — its 2-norm
is its largest singular value — gives

$$
\|A^{-1}\| = \frac{1}{\sigma_{\min}}.
$$ {#eq-invnorm-is-sigmin}

## Defining the condition number {#sec-define}

::: {#def-cond}
**Definition (condition number).** For an invertible matrix $A$, the (2-norm) **condition
number** is

$$
\kappa(A) = \|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\|.
$$ {#eq-cond-def}
:::

Combining the definition with @eq-norm-is-sigmax and @eq-invnorm-is-sigmin gives the form
in the Core, with every step shown:

$$
\kappa(A) = \|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\| = \sigma_{\max}\cdot\frac{1}{\sigma_{\min}}
= \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\min}}.
$$ {#eq-cond-sv}

Three facts follow directly, and each earns its keep later.

- **$\kappa(A) \ge 1$ always.** Since $\sigma_{\max} \ge \sigma_{\min}$, the ratio is at
  least $1$. The floor $\kappa = 1$ is achieved exactly by orthonormal matrices (rotations
  and reflections), where every singular value equals $1$ — they stretch nothing, so they
  amplify no error. These are the *perfectly conditioned* maps.
- **$\kappa$ is scale-invariant.** Multiplying $A$ by a constant $c$ multiplies every
  singular value by $|c|$, so the ratio is unchanged: $\kappa(cA) = \kappa(A)$. Conditioning
  is about the *shape* of the ellipse, not its overall size — exactly right, since a uniform
  rescaling of a problem should not change how trustworthy its answer is.
- **$\kappa \to \infty$ as $A \to$ singular.** A singular matrix has $\sigma_{\min} = 0$
  (its ellipse is flattened completely onto a lower-dimensional subspace), so $\kappa$
  diverges. **Near-singular** matrices have a tiny-but-nonzero $\sigma_{\min}$ and a
  correspondingly enormous $\kappa$. This is the precise sense in which $\kappa$ is a
  *continuous* measure of "how close to singular," where the determinant (@sec-det) is not.

::: {#def-cond-spd}
**Definition (condition number, symmetric positive-definite case).** If $A$ is symmetric
positive-definite, its singular values *are* its eigenvalues (both equal the absolute
values of the eigenvalues, and PD eigenvalues are positive — see below), so

$$
\kappa(A) = \frac{\lambda_{\max}}{\lambda_{\min}},
$$ {#eq-cond-eig}

the ratio of the largest to the smallest eigenvalue.
:::

Why the singular values equal the eigenvalues for symmetric PD $A$: by the spectral theorem
([GEOM-16]) such an $A$ factors as $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ with $Q$ orthonormal and $\Lambda$
the diagonal of positive eigenvalues $\lambda_i > 0$ ([GEOM-18]). That is *already* an SVD —
$U = V = Q$ and $\Sigma = \Lambda$ — because the $\lambda_i$ are nonnegative, which is what
singular values must be. Hence $\sigma_i = \lambda_i$, and the ratio in @eq-cond-sv becomes
the ratio in @eq-cond-eig. This is the form that appears for covariance matrices, Gram
matrices $A^\top A$, and Hessians — all symmetric PSD — so it is the one most often used in
practice.

## The main theorem: how error is amplified {#sec-main-theorem}

Now we prove the claim in the Core. Suppose we solve $Ax = b$, but the right-hand side is
perturbed to $b + \delta b$ (the rounding in $b$, or measurement noise, or both). The
computed answer is then $x + \delta x$, where $A(x + \delta x) = b + \delta b$. We want to
bound the *relative* error in the answer, $\|\delta x\|/\|x\|$, in terms of the relative
error in the data, $\|\delta b\|/\|b\|$.

::: {#thm-cond}
**Theorem (condition number bounds the amplification).** Let $A$ be invertible, let
$Ax = b$ with $b \neq 0$, and let $A(x+\delta x) = b + \delta b$. Then

$$
\frac{\|\delta x\|}{\|x\|} \;\le\; \kappa(A)\,\frac{\|\delta b\|}{\|b\|},
$$ {#eq-cond-bound}

and the bound is **sharp**: there exist perturbations $\delta b$ (and right-hand sides $b$)
for which equality holds.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Step 1 — isolate the error.** Subtract the exact system $Ax = b$ from the perturbed
system $A(x+\delta x) = b + \delta b$. The matrix is the same, so linearity ($A(x+\delta x)
= Ax + A\,\delta x$) gives

$$
A\,\delta x = \delta b
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\delta x = A^{-1}\,\delta b.
$$

The error in the answer is the *inverse* map applied to the error in the data — exactly the
"solving = applying $A^{-1}$" reading from @sec-intuition.

**Step 2 — bound the error from above.** Apply the defining inequality @eq-submult to the
matrix $A^{-1}$:

$$
\|\delta x\| = \|A^{-1}\,\delta b\| \le \|A^{-1}\|\,\|\delta b\|.
$$ {#eq-step2}

**Step 3 — bound the answer's size from below.** We are dividing by $\|x\|$, so we need
$\|x\|$ to be *at least* something, i.e. a lower bound. Start from $b = Ax$ and apply
@eq-submult again, this time to $A$:

$$
\|b\| = \|Ax\| \le \|A\|\,\|x\|
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\|x\| \ge \frac{\|b\|}{\|A\|},
$$ {#eq-step3}

where the rearrangement divides both sides by the positive number $\|A\|$ (which is
positive because $A$ is invertible, so $\sigma_{\max} > 0$).

**Step 4 — assemble.** Take the ratio $\|\delta x\|/\|x\|$ and substitute the upper bound
@eq-step2 for the numerator and the lower bound @eq-step3 for the denominator. Replacing the
numerator by something larger and the denominator by something smaller can only *increase*
the fraction, so the inequality runs the right way:

$$
\frac{\|\delta x\|}{\|x\|}
\;\le\; \frac{\|A^{-1}\|\,\|\delta b\|}{\;\|b\|/\|A\|\;}
= \|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\|\;\frac{\|\delta b\|}{\|b\|}
= \kappa(A)\,\frac{\|\delta b\|}{\|b\|},
$$

using $\kappa(A) = \|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\|$ from @eq-cond-def. That is @eq-cond-bound.

**Step 5 — sharpness.** The two inequalities we used each become equality for a *specific*
choice of vectors, and we can satisfy both at once. Inequality @eq-step3 is tight when
$\|Ax\| = \|A\|\,\|x\|$, i.e. when $x$ points along the most-stretched singular direction
$v_{\max}$ (the right singular vector for $\sigma_{\max}$), so that $b = Ax$ points along
$u_{\max}$. Inequality @eq-step2 is tight when $\|A^{-1}\delta b\| = \|A^{-1}\|\,\|\delta b\|$,
i.e. when $\delta b$ points along the direction $A^{-1}$ stretches most — which is
$u_{\min}$, the left singular vector for $\sigma_{\min}$ (recall $A^{-1}$'s largest stretch
is $1/\sigma_{\min}$, along that axis). Choosing $b \parallel u_{\max}$ and
$\delta b \parallel u_{\min}$ makes both steps exact, so the overall bound is attained:
$\|\delta x\|/\|x\| = \kappa(A)\,\|\delta b\|/\|b\|$. Hence $\kappa$ is not a loose
overestimate — it is the genuine worst case. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Read the theorem in plain words: **relative error in the answer can be as large as $\kappa$
times the relative error in the data.** If your data are good to a relative $10^{-16}$ (the
best a `double` can do) and $\kappa = 10^{10}$, your answer is only guaranteed good to
$10^{-6}$ — you have lost ten of your sixteen digits, purely to the shape of $A$, before
worrying about how the solving algorithm itself behaves. The sharpness clause is what makes
this a warning and not a curiosity: there really is a direction of perturbation that
achieves the worst case, and on a computer the rounding errors do not politely avoid it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"There's a famous rule of thumb — 'you lose $\log_{10}\kappa$ digits.' Where does it come
from?"* Directly from @thm-cond. Floating-point arithmetic introduces relative errors of
size about the unit roundoff $u \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-16}$ (for `double`). The theorem
says these can be amplified to a relative error of about $\kappa\,u$ in the solution. The
number of correct decimal digits is roughly $-\log_{10}(\text{relative error})$, so you
start with about $16$ digits and the amplification costs you $\log_{10}\kappa$ of them:

$$
\text{correct digits} \;\approx\; 16 - \log_{10}\kappa(A).
$$

A condition number of $10^{8}$ costs $8$ digits — half your precision. A condition number
of $10^{16}$ costs all of them: the answer can be pure noise even though $A$ is, on paper,
invertible. This one line is the most useful thing to remember from the whole article.
:::

## Watching the bound bite {#sec-watch-bound}

The theorem is abstract; let us see it happen. We take a $2\times 2$ system with a chosen
condition number, perturb $b$ by a tiny random amount, and measure the relative error that
actually results — then compare it to the bound $\kappa\,\|\delta b\|/\|b\|$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-amplify
#| fig-cap: 'The amplification bound in action. For each target condition number $\kappa$ (horizontal axis), we solve $Ax=b$, then re-solve with $b$ perturbed by a relative $10^{-12}$ in many random directions, and record the *largest* relative error in $x$ that results (red points). The grey line is the theoretical worst case $\kappa \times 10^{-12}$ from @thm-cond. The observed errors hug the bound from below and reach it for the worst-aligned perturbations — confirming both that $\kappa$ is an upper bound and that it is *sharp*. The lesson: error grows linearly with $\kappa$, decade for decade.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
kappas = np.logspace(1, 12, 12)
rel_input = 1e-12
worst_observed = []
for k in kappas:
    # Build a 2x2 with singular values 1 and 1/k via random orthonormal U, V.
    Qu, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((2, 2)))
    Qv, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((2, 2)))
    A = Qu @ np.diag([1.0, 1.0/k]) @ Qv.T
    x_true = rng.standard_normal(2)
    b = A @ x_true
    worst = 0.0
    for _ in range(400):
        d = rng.standard_normal(2)
        db = rel_input * np.linalg.norm(b) * d / np.linalg.norm(d)
        x_pert = np.linalg.solve(A, b + db)
        rel_err = np.linalg.norm(x_pert - x_true) / np.linalg.norm(x_true)
        worst = max(worst, rel_err)
    worst_observed.append(worst)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.loglog(kappas, kappas * rel_input, color=GREY, lw=2.0,
          label=r"bound  $\kappa \times 10^{-12}$")
ax.loglog(kappas, worst_observed, "o", color=HILITE, ms=7,
          label="worst observed relative error")
ax.set_xlabel(r"condition number $\kappa(A)$")
ax.set_ylabel("relative error in solution")
ax.set_title("Error in the answer scales with $\\kappa$")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The observed worst-case error tracks the line $\kappa\times 10^{-12}$ across eleven orders
of magnitude. A relative input error of $10^{-12}$ produces a relative *output* error near
$10^{-2}$ when $\kappa = 10^{10}$: two-thirds of the answer's significant figures are gone,
exactly as the digit-loss rule predicts.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run conditioning on real, honest data — including a case at the edge where the
diagnosis itself becomes delicate.

## Example 1 — the Hilbert matrix: ill-conditioned by construction {#sec-hilbert}

The Hilbert matrix $H_n$ with entries $H_{ij} = 1/(i+j-1)$ is the textbook villain of
numerical linear algebra. It arises genuinely — it is the Gram matrix of the monomials
$1, t, t^2, \dots$ on $[0,1]$ under the integral inner product
$\langle f,g\rangle = \int_0^1 f g\,dt$, so it is exactly the matrix you hit if you try to
fit a polynomial by least squares using the raw powers of $t$ as features (a real and
common mistake). It is symmetric positive-definite, perfectly invertible — and its
condition number explodes with size.

```{python}
#| label: fig-hilbert
#| fig-cap: 'The Hilbert matrix $H_n$ (the Gram matrix of monomials, hence what you get fitting a polynomial in raw powers of $t$). **Left:** the condition number $\kappa(H_n)=\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ grows *exponentially* with the size $n$ — note the log scale; by $n=12$ it exceeds $10^{16}$, the precision floor of double arithmetic, so $H_{12}$ is numerically singular though mathematically invertible. **Right:** solving $H_n x = b$ for a known $x$ (all ones), the number of *correct digits* in the computed answer collapses as $n$ grows, tracking the $16-\log_{10}\kappa$ rule (grey) almost exactly.'
def hilbert(n):
    i = np.arange(1, n+1)
    return 1.0 / (i[:, None] + i[None, :] - 1.0)

ns = np.arange(2, 14)
conds, digits = [], []
pred_digits = []
for n in ns:
    H = hilbert(n)
    k = cond2(H)
    conds.append(k)
    x_true = np.ones(n)
    b = H @ x_true
    x_hat = np.linalg.solve(H, b)
    rel_err = np.linalg.norm(x_hat - x_true) / np.linalg.norm(x_true)
    digits.append(-np.log10(max(rel_err, 1e-17)))
    pred_digits.append(max(16 - np.log10(k), 0))

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.9))
axL.semilogy(ns, conds, "o-", color=VEC)
axL.axhline(1e16, color=HILITE, ls="--", lw=1.5)
axL.text(2.2, 2e16, "double-precision floor $\\sim 10^{16}$", color=HILITE, fontsize=9)
axL.set_xlabel("matrix size $n$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$\kappa(H_n)$")
axL.set_title("Condition number explodes")

axR.plot(ns, digits, "o-", color=VEC, label="correct digits (observed)")
axR.plot(ns, pred_digits, color=GREY, lw=2.0, label=r"$16 - \log_{10}\kappa$")
axR.set_xlabel("matrix size $n$"); axR.set_ylabel("correct digits in solution")
axR.set_title("Precision collapses with size")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

By $n = 10$ the condition number is around $10^{13}$, and the computed solution to
$H_{10}x = b$ — a system whose true answer we *know* is the all-ones vector — is correct to
only two or three digits. Nothing went wrong with the algorithm; `numpy.linalg.solve` uses a
backward-stable LU factorization. The problem is hopeless before the solver starts, and the
digit-loss rule called it exactly. **The practical lesson is not "avoid Hilbert matrices" —
it is "never fit a polynomial in raw powers."** Use an orthogonal basis (Chebyshev or
Legendre polynomials), whose Gram matrix is the identity or nearly so, $\kappa \approx 1$.
Same fit, conditioning fixed at the source.

## Example 2 — multicollinearity: the same disease in regression {#sec-collinearity}

This is the example the rest of the series cares about most. In regression we solve the
**normal equations** $X^\top X\,\hat\beta = X^\top y$ (built in [REGR-05]; geometrically,
least squares is projection — [GEOM-06]). The matrix is the Gram matrix $X^\top X$, and its
condition number is what decides whether the fitted coefficients are trustworthy. When two
predictor columns are nearly **collinear** — one is almost a linear combination of others —
the columns of $X$ point in nearly the same direction, the smallest singular value of $X$
is tiny, and $\kappa(X^\top X)$ is enormous. That is **multicollinearity**, and conditioning
is its exact mathematical content.

We build a small honest design: two features that are *almost* the same (say, height in
centimeters and height in centimeters-plus-tiny-noise — the kind of accidental near-duplicate
that creeps into real feature sets), plus a clean third feature, and watch the coefficients
swing.

```{python}
#| label: fig-collinear
#| fig-cap: 'Multicollinearity is ill-conditioning. We regress an outcome on three features, where features 1 and 2 are nearly identical (correlation $\approx 0.999$). **Left:** the design''s near-collinear columns make $X^\top X$ ill-conditioned ($\kappa$ shown). **Right:** refitting on 200 bootstrap resamples of the data, the estimated coefficients for the two collinear features (red, gold) scatter wildly — and in mirror image, one large-positive whenever the other is large-negative — while the clean feature''s coefficient (green) stays put. The model cannot tell the two near-duplicates apart, so it trades enormous coefficients back and forth between them; tiny data wiggles swing the answer, exactly as $\kappa$ predicts.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
n = 120
f1 = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
f2 = f1 + rng.normal(0, 0.03, n)        # near-duplicate of f1
f3 = rng.normal(0, 1, n)               # clean, independent feature
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), f1, f2, f3])
beta_true = np.array([1.0, 2.0, -1.0, 3.0])
y = X @ beta_true + rng.normal(0, 0.5, n)

kappa_XtX = cond2(X.T @ X)

# Bootstrap the fit.
B = 200
coefs = np.zeros((B, 4))
for bdx in range(B):
    idx = rng.integers(0, n, n)
    Xb, yb = X[idx], y[idx]
    coefs[bdx] = np.linalg.lstsq(Xb, yb, rcond=None)[0]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.9))
# correlation heat between the three features
C = np.corrcoef(np.column_stack([f1, f2, f3]).T)
im = axL.imshow(C, cmap="RdBu_r", vmin=-1, vmax=1)
axL.set_xticks([0,1,2]); axL.set_yticks([0,1,2])
axL.set_xticklabels(["f1","f2","f3"]); axL.set_yticklabels(["f1","f2","f3"])
for i in range(3):
    for j in range(3):
        axL.text(j, i, f"{C[i,j]:.2f}", ha="center", va="center",
                 color="white" if abs(C[i,j])>0.5 else "black", fontsize=10)
axL.set_title(f"feature correlations\n$\\kappa(X^\\top X)={kappa_XtX:.1e}$")
axL.grid(False)

axR.scatter(coefs[:, 1], coefs[:, 2], color=HILITE, s=18, alpha=0.6,
            label="(f1 coef, f2 coef)")
axR.scatter(coefs[:, 3], np.zeros(B), color=ACCENT, s=18, alpha=0.6,
            label="f3 coef (on axis)")
axR.axvline(beta_true[1], color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.2)
axR.axhline(beta_true[2], color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.2)
axR.set_xlabel("coefficient value (f1, or f3)")
axR.set_ylabel("f2 coefficient")
axR.set_title("Collinear coefs swing wildly\n(clean coef is stable)")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"kappa(X)      = {cond2(X):.3e}")
print(f"kappa(X^T X)  = {kappa_XtX:.3e}   (note: roughly the square of kappa(X))")
print(f"f1 coef: mean {coefs[:,1].mean():6.2f}  sd {coefs[:,1].std():6.2f}  (true {beta_true[1]})")
print(f"f2 coef: mean {coefs[:,2].mean():6.2f}  sd {coefs[:,2].std():6.2f}  (true {beta_true[2]})")
print(f"f3 coef: mean {coefs[:,3].mean():6.2f}  sd {coefs[:,3].std():6.2f}  (true {beta_true[3]})")
```

The clean feature's coefficient is estimated tightly around its true value; the two
near-duplicate features' coefficients have enormous standard deviations and are
anti-correlated — the fit shovels weight from one to the other because the data cannot
distinguish them. This is not a bug in least squares; it is conditioning. The cure is not a
better solver but a better *problem*: drop one of the duplicates, combine them, or **add a
ridge penalty**, which we explain next as a conditioning fix.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How does ridge regression help, in conditioning terms?"* Ridge solves
$(X^\top X + \lambda I)\hat\beta = X^\top y$ instead of $X^\top X\hat\beta = X^\top y$. The
eigenvalues of $X^\top X$ are the $\sigma_i(X)^2$ (shown in @sec-normal-eqs-squaring), with
some orthonormal eigenvectors $v_i$, so $X^\top X\,v_i = \sigma_i^2\,v_i$. Adding $\lambda I$
shifts *every* eigenvalue up by $\lambda$ while keeping the *same* eigenvectors — apply the
shifted matrix to the same $v_i$:

$$
(X^\top X + \lambda I)\,v_i = X^\top X\,v_i + \lambda v_i
= \sigma_i^2\,v_i + \lambda v_i = (\sigma_i^2 + \lambda)\,v_i,
$$

so $v_i$ is still an eigenvector, now with eigenvalue $\sigma_i^2 + \lambda$. The shifted
matrix is still symmetric PD (its eigenvalues $\sigma_i^2 + \lambda > 0$), so the SPD form
@eq-cond-eig applies and its condition number is the ratio of the largest to the smallest of
these shifted eigenvalues:

$$
\kappa(X^\top X + \lambda I) = \frac{\sigma_{\max}^2 + \lambda}{\sigma_{\min}^2 + \lambda}.
$$

This is *smaller* than the unregularized $\sigma_{\max}^2/\sigma_{\min}^2$ for any
$\lambda > 0$. To see it, cross-multiply the two positive fractions: the claim
$\frac{\sigma_{\max}^2 + \lambda}{\sigma_{\min}^2 + \lambda} < \frac{\sigma_{\max}^2}{\sigma_{\min}^2}$
is equivalent to $\sigma_{\min}^2(\sigma_{\max}^2 + \lambda) < \sigma_{\max}^2(\sigma_{\min}^2 + \lambda)$,
i.e. $\sigma_{\min}^2\sigma_{\max}^2 + \lambda\sigma_{\min}^2 < \sigma_{\min}^2\sigma_{\max}^2 + \lambda\sigma_{\max}^2$,
i.e. $\lambda\sigma_{\min}^2 < \lambda\sigma_{\max}^2$ — true whenever $\lambda > 0$ and
$\sigma_{\min} < \sigma_{\max}$. As $\lambda \to \infty$ both shifted eigenvalues are
dominated by $\lambda$ and the ratio tends to $1$. The tiny $\sigma_{\min}^2$ that was wrecking the conditioning is
lifted off the floor. That is the linear-algebra reason ridge stabilizes the swinging
coefficients of @fig-collinear — bought at the price of a small bias. The statistical
bias–variance side of that trade is owned by [REGR-14]; the *conditioning* side is this
article's.
:::

## Example 3 — the edge: estimating $\kappa$ when you can't see $\sigma_{\min}$ {#sec-edge}

Here is the honest edge case. The bound is clean and the cure is clear — *when you know
$\kappa$*. But on a genuinely ill-conditioned matrix, computing $\kappa$ itself is delicate,
because $\sigma_{\min}$ is exactly the quantity that floating-point arithmetic struggles to
pin down: it is a small number obtained by subtracting nearly-equal large numbers, the
classic recipe for **catastrophic cancellation**. So the diagnostic shares the disease it
diagnoses.

We demonstrate by forming a matrix with a *known* tiny smallest singular value, then asking
two routines for $\kappa$: a direct SVD of $A$, versus the tempting shortcut of going
through $A^\top A$ (whose condition number is $\kappa^2$, so its $\sigma_{\min}$ is squared
and even harder to see).

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: 'Estimating $\kappa$ is itself ill-conditioned. We build matrices with a *known* condition number (horizontal axis) and estimate it two ways. The SVD of $A$ directly (green) stays accurate far longer. Routing through $A^\top A$ (red) — i.e. estimating $\kappa(A) = \sqrt{\kappa(A^\top A)}$ — squares the condition number internally, so its estimate of $\sigma_{\min}$ drowns in roundoff and the reported $\kappa$ saturates and then collapses to garbage once $\kappa^2$ pierces the precision floor near $\kappa \approx 10^8$. The diagnostic inherits the disease: when a matrix is badly conditioned, even *measuring how badly* is hard, and you must never square the problem to do it.'
true_kappas = np.logspace(1, 15, 22)
est_svd, est_gram = [], []
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
for k in true_kappas:
    Qu, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((6, 6)))
    Qv, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((6, 6)))
    s = np.logspace(0, -np.log10(k), 6)   # singular values from 1 down to 1/k
    A = Qu @ np.diag(s) @ Qv.T
    est_svd.append(cond2(A))
    # via the Gram matrix: kappa(A) = sqrt(kappa(A^T A))
    est_gram.append(np.sqrt(cond2(A.T @ A)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.loglog(true_kappas, true_kappas, color=GREY, lw=1.6, ls="--",
          label="perfect estimate (y = x)")
ax.loglog(true_kappas, est_svd, "o-", color=ACCENT, ms=5,
          label=r"SVD of $A$ directly")
ax.loglog(true_kappas, est_gram, "s-", color=HILITE, ms=5,
          label=r"via $A^\top A$ (squares $\kappa$)")
ax.set_xlabel(r"true condition number $\kappa(A)$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"estimated $\kappa(A)$")
ax.set_title("Even measuring $\\kappa$ is ill-conditioned")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The direct SVD estimate stays on the diagonal up to about $\kappa = 10^{15}$; the
through-the-Gram-matrix estimate departs near $\kappa = 10^{8}$, because squaring the
condition number internally pushes its effective $\sigma_{\min}$ below the precision floor.
This is the same phenomenon — *squaring $\kappa$ doubles the digit loss* — that makes the
normal equations a poor way to solve least squares (@sec-normal-eqs-squaring), now seen in
the act of *measuring* the condition number. The edge-of-applicability message: $\kappa$ is
the right diagnostic, but compute it from the SVD of $A$, never from $A^\top A$, and treat a
reported $\kappa$ near $10^{16}$ as "numerically singular — I cannot even tell you how
singular."

# Conditioning versus stability, and the floating-point rules {#sec-stability}

We have a number for the *problem*. The remaining honest content is about *algorithms*: how
not to make a well-conditioned problem worse, and three rules that follow.

## The two separate notions {#sec-two-notions}

::: {.callout-important title="Conditioning is the problem; stability is the algorithm"}
**Conditioning** ($\kappa$) measures how sensitive the *true* answer is to perturbations in
the *true* data — a property of $A$ alone. **Stability** measures how much *extra* error a
particular algorithm introduces beyond what conditioning forces. The gold standard is
**backward stability**: a backward-stable algorithm returns the exact answer to a slightly
perturbed problem (one within relative $\sim u$ of the original). For such an algorithm the
total error is bounded by (roughly) $\kappa \cdot u$ — conditioning times machine precision
— and *nothing more*. The full error budget is therefore:

$$
\underbrace{\text{computed-answer error}}_{\text{what you see}}
\;\approx\;
\underbrace{\kappa(A)}_{\text{conditioning (problem)}}
\;\times\;
\underbrace{u}_{\text{precision (}\sim 10^{-16}\text{)}}
\;\times\;
\underbrace{(\text{stability constant})}_{\text{algorithm}}.
$$

You cannot beat $\kappa \cdot u$ — that is forced by the problem. A *good* algorithm keeps
the stability constant modest (a small polynomial in the matrix size); a *bad* algorithm
inflates it, throwing away accuracy a well-conditioned problem did not have to lose.
:::

The three practical rules below are all instances of "do not let the algorithm inflate the
error beyond $\kappa u$."

## Rule 1 — solve, don't invert {#sec-solve-dont-invert}

The instinct on seeing $Ax = b$ is to write $x = A^{-1}b$ and compute $A^{-1}$ explicitly.
**Don't.** Forming $A^{-1}$ and then multiplying is both slower (it does more arithmetic
than necessary) and less accurate (it accumulates more rounding) than directly factoring
$A$ and solving. Library calls like `numpy.linalg.solve(A, b)` perform an LU factorization
($A = PLU$) and two triangular solves — backward-stable, and it never forms $A^{-1}$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solve-vs-invert
#| fig-cap: 'Solve, don''t invert. For random matrices across a range of condition numbers, we solve $Ax=b$ two ways and measure the relative error against a high-precision reference: `solve(A,b)` (green) versus forming `inv(A) @ b` (red). Both degrade as $\kappa$ grows — neither can beat the conditioning floor — but explicit inversion is consistently worse by a modest factor, having spent extra arithmetic (and extra rounding) to produce a full inverse it then mostly discards. Same answer in theory; fewer correct digits in practice.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
kappas = np.logspace(1, 13, 13)
err_solve, err_inv = [], []
for k in kappas:
    Qu, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((8, 8)))
    Qv, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((8, 8)))
    s = np.logspace(0, -np.log10(k), 8)
    A = Qu @ np.diag(s) @ Qv.T
    x_true = rng.standard_normal(8)
    b = A @ x_true
    x_solve = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
    x_inv = np.linalg.inv(A) @ b
    err_solve.append(np.linalg.norm(x_solve - x_true) / np.linalg.norm(x_true))
    err_inv.append(np.linalg.norm(x_inv - x_true) / np.linalg.norm(x_true))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.loglog(kappas, err_solve, "o-", color=ACCENT, label="solve(A, b)")
ax.loglog(kappas, err_inv, "s-", color=HILITE, label="inv(A) @ b")
ax.loglog(kappas, kappas * 1e-16, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4,
          label=r"$\kappa \cdot u$ floor")
ax.set_xlabel(r"condition number $\kappa(A)$")
ax.set_ylabel("relative error")
ax.set_title("Solving beats inverting")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The deeper reason to never form $A^{-1}$: you almost never actually *want* the inverse. You
want $A^{-1}b$ (a solve), or $A^{-1}B$ (several solves), or a quadratic form $b^\top A^{-1}b$
(a solve then a dot product). Every one of these is computed more accurately and more
cheaply by factoring once and solving, than by building the inverse. The explicit $A^{-1}$
is a tool you reach for far less often than the notation suggests.

## Rule 2 — the determinant is a terrible singularity test {#sec-det}

It is tempting to test "is $A$ singular / ill-conditioned?" by checking whether
$\det A \approx 0$. This is wrong, and the reason is scale. The determinant is the *product*
of the singular values (up to sign), $|\det A| = \sigma_1\sigma_2\cdots\sigma_n =
\prod_i \sigma_i$ — it measures the *volume* scaling of $A$ ([GEOM-13]), not its *shape*.
Two failures follow.

First, **a perfectly conditioned matrix can have a tiny determinant.** Take
$A = 0.01\, I_{100}$ — a hundred-dimensional identity scaled by $1/100$. Every singular value
is $0.01$, so $\kappa = 0.01/0.01 = 1$, perfectly conditioned. But its determinant is
$0.01^{100} = 10^{-200}$, underflowing to exactly zero in floating point. By the determinant
test it is "singular"; by conditioning it is the *best-behaved matrix there is*. The
determinant was fooled by overall scale, which conditioning correctly ignores
(@sec-define, scale-invariance).

Second, **a nearly singular matrix can have a large determinant** — pile up many singular
values around $10$ but let one be $10^{-6}$, and the product is still large while $\kappa$ is
huge. The determinant mixes all $n$ singular values into one product and so cannot isolate
the single small one that conditioning hangs on.

```{python}
#| label: fig-det-bad
#| fig-cap: 'The determinant lies about conditioning. Diagonal matrices $cI_{10}$ for a range of scales $c$: the determinant $c^{10}$ (red) sweeps across 100 orders of magnitude and crosses zero-to-the-eye, while the condition number (green) is *exactly $1$ for every one of them* — these are all perfectly conditioned. A near-zero determinant signals smallness of scale, not closeness to singularity. Use $\sigma_{\min}$ (or $\kappa$, or the rank from the SVD), never $\det$.'
scales = np.logspace(-3, 1.5, 30)
dets = scales ** 10
conds = np.ones_like(scales)   # cI has all singular values = c, so kappa = 1
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.loglog(scales, np.maximum(dets, 1e-40), "o-", color=HILITE,
          label=r"$|\det(cI_{10})| = c^{10}$")
ax.loglog(scales, conds, "s-", color=ACCENT, label=r"$\kappa(cI_{10}) = 1$ (always)")
ax.set_xlabel("scale $c$"); ax.set_ylabel("value (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Determinant tracks scale, not conditioning")
ax.legend(loc="center left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The right tests are: the **smallest singular value** $\sigma_{\min}$ (zero $\Rightarrow$
singular, tiny $\Rightarrow$ ill-conditioned), the **condition number** $\kappa$, or the
**numerical rank** (count of singular values above a tolerance) — all read straight off the
SVD. The determinant answers a different question (volume) and should never be drafted for
this one.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Never test for singularity or ill-conditioning with `det(A) ≈ 0`.** The determinant is
the product of the singular values, so it conflates *scale* with *near-degeneracy*: a
well-conditioned matrix can have a determinant of $10^{-200}$ (and underflow to zero), and a
badly-conditioned matrix can have a healthy-looking determinant. Worse, computing $\det$ of
a large matrix is itself error-prone. Ask the SVD for $\sigma_{\min}$ or $\kappa$, or ask
for the numerical rank — those measure the thing you actually care about.
:::

## Rule 3 — the normal equations square the condition number {#sec-normal-eqs-squaring}

The most consequential rule for the regression track. To solve least squares
$\min_\beta \|X\beta - y\|^2$, the textbook route forms the normal equations
$X^\top X\,\beta = X^\top y$ and solves that square system. It works in exact arithmetic.
But on a computer it can lose *twice* as many digits as necessary, because forming
$X^\top X$ **squares the condition number**.

Here is the complete reason, every step shown. The singular values of $X$ are
$\sigma_1 \ge \dots \ge \sigma_n$, so $\kappa(X) = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$. The Gram
matrix $X^\top X$ is symmetric PSD; its eigenvalues are the *squares* of $X$'s singular
values. (Why: write $X = U\Sigma V^\top$; then
$X^\top X = V\Sigma^\top U^\top U\Sigma V^\top = V\Sigma^\top\Sigma V^\top = V\Sigma^2 V^\top$,
since $U^\top U = I$. That is a spectral decomposition of $X^\top X$ with eigenvalues the
diagonal of $\Sigma^2$, namely the $\sigma_i^2$.) So, using the SPD form @eq-cond-eig,

$$
\kappa(X^\top X) = \frac{\sigma_{\max}^2}{\sigma_{\min}^2}
= \left(\frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\min}}\right)^{\!2}
= \kappa(X)^2.
$$ {#eq-square}

By the digit-loss rule, this **doubles the digits lost**: if $X$ has $\kappa(X) = 10^{6}$ —
a moderately collinear design that, solved well, would cost six digits — then forming
$X^\top X$ gives $\kappa = 10^{12}$ and costs twelve. You can erase six good digits purely
by choosing the squaring algorithm.

The fix is to **never form $X^\top X$**. Factor $X$ directly with **QR** ([GEOM-07]:
$X = QR$, $Q$ orthonormal, $R$ upper-triangular) and solve $R\beta = Q^\top y$, which has
condition number $\kappa(R) = \kappa(X)$ — the original, not its square. Or use the **SVD**
([GEOM-20]) and the pseudoinverse ([GEOM-21]), which is the most robust of all and degrades
most gracefully on rank-deficient designs. Both work directly with $X$ and inherit only
$\kappa(X)$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-normal-vs-qr
#| fig-cap: 'Normal equations square the condition number. We solve the *same* least-squares problems three ways across designs of increasing $\kappa(X)$: the normal equations $X^\top X\beta = X^\top y$ (red), QR ($R\beta = Q^\top y$, green), and the SVD/pseudoinverse (blue). QR and SVD track the $\kappa(X)\cdot u$ floor (grey); the normal equations track $\kappa(X)^2\cdot u$ — they fail roughly when $\kappa(X)$ reaches $10^8$ (so $\kappa(X)^2$ hits the precision floor), losing the answer while QR and SVD still have half their digits. This is *the* reason production least-squares solvers use QR or SVD, never the normal equations, despite the latter''s textbook prominence.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
kappas = np.logspace(1, 12, 12)
err_ne, err_qr, err_svd = [], [], []
m = 40
for k in kappas:
    # Build a 40x6 design with prescribed kappa(X).
    Qu, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((m, 6)))
    Qv, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((6, 6)))
    s = np.logspace(0, -np.log10(k), 6)
    X = Qu @ np.diag(s) @ Qv.T
    beta_true = rng.standard_normal(6)
    y = X @ beta_true                      # exact, so the only error is numerical
    # normal equations
    b_ne = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ y)
    # QR
    Q, R = np.linalg.qr(X)
    b_qr = np.linalg.solve(R, Q.T @ y)
    # SVD / pseudoinverse
    b_svd = np.linalg.pinv(X) @ y
    err_ne.append(np.linalg.norm(b_ne - beta_true) / np.linalg.norm(beta_true))
    err_qr.append(np.linalg.norm(b_qr - beta_true) / np.linalg.norm(beta_true))
    err_svd.append(np.linalg.norm(b_svd - beta_true) / np.linalg.norm(beta_true))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.loglog(kappas, np.maximum(err_ne, 1e-18), "s-", color=HILITE,
          label=r"normal equations ($\kappa^2$)")
ax.loglog(kappas, np.maximum(err_qr, 1e-18), "o-", color=ACCENT, label="QR")
ax.loglog(kappas, np.maximum(err_svd, 1e-18), "^-", color=VEC, label="SVD / pinv")
ax.loglog(kappas, kappas * 1e-16, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3, label=r"$\kappa \cdot u$")
ax.loglog(kappas, (kappas**2) * 1e-16, color=GOLD, ls=":", lw=1.6, label=r"$\kappa^2 \cdot u$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"condition number $\kappa(X)$")
ax.set_ylabel("relative error in $\\hat\\beta$")
ax.set_title("QR/SVD beat the normal equations")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The normal-equations error rides the $\kappa^2$ line and falls off the precision cliff a
full square-root sooner than QR and SVD, which ride the $\kappa$ line. The two summit
factorizations of this track ([GEOM-07]'s QR and [GEOM-20]'s SVD) are not academic
ornaments; they are the *reason* a well-built least-squares solver keeps its digits.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If the normal equations are so bad, why are they in every textbook?"* Because they are the
cleanest *mathematical* statement of least squares — $X^\top X\beta = X^\top y$ falls
straight out of setting the gradient of $\|X\beta - y\|^2$ to zero ([GEOM-25]), and the
geometry (residual orthogonal to the column space, [GEOM-06]) lives there. They are the right
object for *deriving* and *understanding* least squares. They are the wrong object for
*computing* it in finite precision, precisely because of @eq-square. The mature view: derive
with the normal equations, compute with QR or SVD. Knowing *both* — and why they differ — is
exactly what an interviewer is probing.
:::

# Where conditioning bites elsewhere {#sec-elsewhere}

The condition number is not a regression-only concern; it is the universal currency of
numerical trustworthiness, and three quick connections show its reach.

- **Optimization.** For gradient descent on a quadratic bowl $f(x) = \tfrac12 x^\top A x -
  b^\top x$ ([GEOM-25]), the number of iterations to converge scales with $\kappa(A)$ — the
  ratio of the steepest to the flattest curvature, the eigenvalue ratio of the Hessian
  ([GEOM-24]). A round bowl ($\kappa = 1$) is solved in one step; an elongated valley (huge
  $\kappa$) makes gradient descent zig-zag agonizingly down a near-flat trough. *The same
  ellipse, now governing a different cost.* This is why **preconditioning** — left-multiplying
  to reduce $\kappa$ — and why Newton's method (which rescales by $A^{-1}$ and so sees
  $\kappa = 1$) exist. Optimization owns the algorithms; it consumes this conditioning story.

- **Eigenvalue problems** have their own conditioning, distinct from the linear-solve
  $\kappa$: a single eigenvalue's sensitivity depends on how nearly *parallel* the
  eigenvectors are (how far the matrix is from being normal), not on $\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$.
  We flag this so you do not over-extend the linear-solve $\kappa$ to a problem it does not
  govern — a common interview trap.

- **Polynomial and Vandermonde systems**, signal-processing inverse problems, and PDE
  discretizations are all routinely ill-conditioned, and all are diagnosed and treated with
  the same toolkit: estimate $\sigma_{\min}$, refuse to invert, factor stably, and if the
  problem itself is hopeless, *regularize* (the ridge move of @sec-collinearity, generalized).

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The honest boundary of conditioning analysis itself.

- **$\kappa$ is a worst case, not a typical case.** @thm-cond bounds the worst alignment of
  the perturbation; a *random* perturbation usually lands partly along benign directions and
  amplifies less. So a large $\kappa$ is a warning, not a verdict — your particular $b$ might
  be fine. The discipline is to treat $\kappa$ as the safe upper bound, because on a computer
  the rounding errors *do* eventually find the worst direction across many operations.

- **$\kappa$ depends on scaling, and bad scaling can be fixed.** If one column of $X$ is in
  meters and another in nanometers, the raw $\kappa$ can be huge for a reason that is pure
  unit choice, not genuine collinearity. **Equilibration** — rescaling rows/columns to
  comparable size (e.g. standardizing features to unit variance, the routine first step in
  regression) — can collapse a spurious $\kappa$ dramatically. Always rule out a scaling
  artifact before declaring a problem intrinsically ill-conditioned.

- **When conditioning analysis does not apply.** The whole framework assumes a *linear* map
  and a meaningful norm. For combinatorial problems, integer programs, or highly nonlinear
  maps, "condition number" must be redefined (the relevant object is the Jacobian's
  conditioning, locally) or abandoned. And for a genuinely rank-deficient problem — a true
  zero singular value, not a small one — there is no unique solution to condition; you have
  left the world of $\kappa$ and entered the world of the **pseudoinverse and minimum-norm
  solutions** ([GEOM-21]), which is the right tool there.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the condition number the right diagnostic, and when is it the wrong one?"* The
honest summary:

- **Use it** to (a) decide whether a linear solve or least-squares fit can be trusted in
  finite precision, (b) explain multicollinearity quantitatively, (c) anticipate
  gradient-descent convergence on a quadratic, and (d) justify regularization. It is the
  single number that turns "this feels unstable" into a prediction with a digit count.
- **Do not lean on it** when the real issue is scaling (equilibrate first), when the problem
  is rank-deficient (use the pseudoinverse — there is no finite $\kappa$), when the map is
  nonlinear (condition the Jacobian, locally), or for eigenvalue sensitivity (a different
  conditioning theory). And never compute it through $\det$ or through $A^\top A$.
- **The deepest point to land:** $\kappa$ describes the *problem*, stability describes the
  *algorithm*, and precision is the *hardware floor*. Total error $\approx \kappa \cdot u
  \cdot (\text{stability})$. You fix conditioning by changing the problem (regularize,
  rescale, reparametrize), and stability by changing the algorithm (QR/SVD, solve-don't-invert)
  — and you cannot fix either with the other.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived with the full geometric toolbox — **eigenstructure** ([GEOM-14]), the **SVD**
([GEOM-20]) and its $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_{\max}$ reading, **positive-definiteness** ([GEOM-18]),
and the **quadratic-form/Hessian** picture ([GEOM-24], [GEOM-25]). This article cashed them
in for the one number — $\kappa = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ — that says whether the
beautiful exact answers survive a real computer. From here:

- **[GEOM-27] — How These Tools Power Data Science (the bridge).** The terminal article maps
  every tool, including this one, to its downstream consumers, and chains several together on
  real data with the honest "where does the geometry break" discussion. Conditioning is the
  reality check that runs underneath all of it.
- **[REGR-07] — Multicollinearity.** This article *discharges* that one: the eigenvalues and
  condition number of $X^\top X$, the swinging coefficients of @fig-collinear, and the ridge
  fix are exactly its content, supplied here as geometry. REGR-07 builds the statistical
  interpretation (variance inflation, confidence-interval blowup) on top and links back.
- **[REGR-14] — Ridge Regression.** Owns the bias–variance trade of the
  $\kappa(X^\top X + \lambda I)$ shrinkage we previewed; the conditioning half is here.
- **Optimization.** Consumes $\kappa$ as the convergence-rate driver for gradient descent and
  the motivation for preconditioning and Newton's method, linking back rather than
  re-deriving the eigenvalue-ratio story.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding analysis.** A. M. Turing, "Rounding-off errors in matrix processes,"
  *Quarterly Journal of Mechanics and Applied Mathematics* 1 (1948) — where condition numbers
  for matrices are introduced. John von Neumann and H. H. Goldstine, "Numerical inverting of
  matrices of high order," *Bulletin of the AMS* 53 (1947), is the companion that forced the
  question into the open.
- **The modern standard reference.** Nick Higham, *Accuracy and Stability of Numerical
  Algorithms* (2nd ed., SIAM 2002) — the definitive treatment of conditioning, backward
  stability, and the error analysis of essentially every algorithm in this article. Lloyd N.
  Trefethen and David Bau, *Numerical Linear Algebra* (SIAM 1997), Lectures 12–18, give the
  cleanest conceptual development of conditioning, stability, and the SVD's role.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *How exactly does $\kappa$ set the convergence rate
  of gradient descent and conjugate gradients?* — the Optimization track (the rate is
  $(\sqrt{\kappa}-1)/(\sqrt{\kappa}+1)$ for conjugate gradients, a $\sqrt{\kappa}$ improvement
  worth its own derivation). *What is the minimum-norm solution when $A$ is rank-deficient and
  $\kappa$ is infinite?* — [GEOM-21], via the pseudoinverse. *How do we estimate $\kappa$
  cheaply for huge matrices without a full SVD?* — condition-number *estimators* (the
  LAPACK `*con` routines), a practical rabbit hole one level below this article. *What is the
  conditioning of the eigenvalue problem, as opposed to the linear solve?* — a separate theory
  keyed to eigenvector non-orthogonality, flagged in @sec-elsewhere.
